draft.introduction: Simplicius of Cilicia (c. 490–560 CE) wrote the most extensive surviving commentary on Aristotle’s Physics. His work preserves invaluable fragments of lost Pre-Socratic texts and demonstrates the late Neoplatonic project of harmonizing Aristotle with Plato.

translation.original_ref: Simplicius, In Aristotelis Physicorum Libros Commentaria 23.21–24.20 (Diels, CAG IX)
translation.text: Anaximander son of Praxiades, a Milesian and successor to Thales, said that the unlimited is the principle and element of existing things. He was the first to introduce this word “principle.” He says it is neither water nor any other of the so-called elements, but some other unlimited nature, from which all the heavens and the worlds within them come into being.
translation.original_text: Ἀναξίμανδρος Πραξιάδου Μιλήσιος, Θαλοῦ γενόμενος διάδοχος καὶ μαθητής

translator_notes.note: Simplicius is our primary source for many Pre-Socratic fragments, including Anaximander’s. His commentary method—extensive verbatim quotation followed by philosophical analysis—has preserved texts that would otherwise be entirely lost. Without Simplicius, much of early Greek philosophy would be unknown.
